cards.text: To create a shared_ptr, use: {{c1::std::shared_ptr<Type> ptr = std::make_shared<Type>(args);}}

cards.front: What ownership model does std::shared_ptr implement?
cards.back: Shared ownership. Multiple shared_ptrs can own the same resource. The resource is deleted when the last shared_ptr owning it is destroyed (reference counting).

cards.text: std::shared_ptr uses {{c1::reference counting}} to track how many shared_ptrs own the resource. When the count reaches {{c2::zero}}, the resource is {{c3::deleted}}.

cards.question: What happens to the reference count in this code?
cards.answer: Count: 1, then 2, then 3, then back to 1 after the block.
cards.explanation: Each copy of shared_ptr increments the reference count. When ptr2 and ptr3 go out of scope, the count decrements. The object is deleted when count reaches 0.

cards.text: Call {{c1::ptr.use_count()}} to get the current reference count of a shared_ptr.

cards.front: Why is std::make_shared preferred over the shared_ptr constructor with new?
cards.back: std::make_shared is more efficient (single allocation for object + control block), exception-safe, and more concise than using new.

cards.concept: When to use std::shared_ptr vs std::unique_ptr
cards.explanation:
Use std::shared_ptr when:
  • Multiple owners need to share ownership
  • Ownership is complex or unclear
  • Object lifetime can't be determined statically
Use std::unique_ptr when:
  • Single, clear ownership
  • Most cases - prefer unique_ptr by default

cards.question: What's the difference in reference count behavior?
cards.answer: Option A increments ref count. Option B transfers ownership without incrementing count (ptr1 becomes null).
cards.explanation: Copying shared_ptr increments reference count. Moving transfers ownership, leaving source null but keeping the same total count.

cards.text: std::shared_ptr has overhead compared to unique_ptr: it stores {{c1::two pointers}} (one to the object, one to the control block) and maintains a {{c2::reference count}}.

cards.front: What thread-safety guarantees does std::shared_ptr provide?
cards.back: The reference count operations are thread-safe (atomic). However, accessing the managed object itself is NOT thread-safe - you need separate synchronization for that.

cards.question: What does reset() do on a shared_ptr?
cards.answer: ptr1 becomes null, reference count decrements to 1. The object is NOT deleted because ptr2 still owns it.
cards.explanation: reset() releases this shared_ptr's ownership. The object is only deleted when ALL shared_ptrs release ownership (count reaches 0).

Pitfalls to avoid:
  • Creating multiple shared_ptrs from same raw pointer (double delete)
  • Circular references (memory leak - use weak_ptr)
  • Unnecessary shared ownership (prefer unique_ptr)
  • Excessive copying (impacts performance)
